elements.sent-2411-001: While it is well established that the dynamics of an open system is described well by the Lindblad master equation if the coupling to the bath is either in the weak or in the singular limit, it is not known whether this description can be extended to some other coupling strength.
elements.sent-2411-002: Here we use the exact solution of a microscopic system coupled to baths, to show that, for a pure decay system, Lindbladian dynamics only holds in the weak and singular coupling limits.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also show that Lindblad and non-Hermitian dynamics are equivalent in the highest particle subspace of such systems with pure decay.
elements.sent-2411-004: Using this equivalence, we rule out the possibility of obtaining non-Hermitian dynamics for any other couplings.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we argue that exceptional points can only occur in the singular coupling limit, never in the weak coupling limit.
elements.sent-2411-006: Physically, this means that exceptional points can occur when the intrinsic time evolution of the isolated system is comparable to the relaxation time scale.. \color{black.}
elements.sent-2411-007: This requires a good understanding of the theoretical frameworks that can be used to accurately describe experimental data \cite{Murch, Murch2, Wineland, Zhang, IBM .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Therefore, an important theoretical goal is to study how to properly `integrate out' the large number of degrees of freedom in a bath model.
elements.sent-2411-009: One well established approach for modelling open systems is the Lindblad Master equation \cite{Lindblad1976, GKS, manzano2020short , which replaces solving the Schr\"odinger equation for the total Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{Open} H=H_A+H_B+H_C\end{equation} where $H_A$, $H_B$ and $H_C$ describe the system, the bath, and their coupling, respectively, with the Lindblad equation for the density matrix of the system \begin{equation}\label{Lindblad} \dot{\rho}_A=L[\rho_A]= -\im [H_A,\rho_A] + \sum_j \Gamma_j \left( L_j \rho_A L_j^\dag -\frac{1}{2} \left\{ L_j^\dag L_j , \rho_A \right\} \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Here $L_j$ are system operators, the gain/decay rates \color{black $\Gamma_j$ vanish when $H_C=0$, and we set $\hbar=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The Lindblad Master equation assumes a Markovian bath and was proven to be accurate (i) in the weak coupling limit, where the energy scale $C$ of the system-bath coupling is much smaller than the energy scales $A, B$ of the system and of the bath; and (ii) in the singular limit where $B/A\rightarrow \infty$ and $C/A\rightarrow \infty$ such that $C^2/B$ is constant \cite{BreuerPetruccione,palmer1977singular .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The Lindblad approach was found to work reasonably well for interpreting experiments \cite{Murch, Murch2, Wineland, Zhang, IBM , but it remains an open question whether it is generically accurate outside the two limits mentioned above.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Another very popular approach for the study of open systems is to assume that the dynamical equation for $\rho_A$ is like the first line of Eq. (\ref{Lindblad ), {\em i.e. like that of a closed system, but with the system Hamiltonian $H_A$ replaced by some non-Hermitian operator $H_{nh}$ \cite{Sato, Bardarson, Nunnenkamp, Lorenzo,Porras, Monkman .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, it is still far from clear when such a non-Hermitian description of an open system is adequate.
elements.sent-2411-015: A direct link between the two approaches is established by noting that if the action of the quantum jumps term \begin{equation}\label{LindbladQuantumJumps} \mathcal{Q}[\rho_A]=\sum_j \Gamma_j L_j \rho_A L_j^\dag\end{equation} can be ignored in Eq. (\ref{Lindblad ), then the Lindblad equation is equivalent to evolution under the non-Hermitian operator \cite{Nori, Bagarello \begin{equation}\label{LindbladHamiltonian} H_{\text{nh}}=H_A -\frac{\im}{2}\sum_j \Gamma_j {L}^\dag_j L_j.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The right eigenvectors of this non-Hermitian operator are not necessarily orthogonal to one another.
elements.sent-2411-017: This can lead to exceptional points where two eigenvectors of $H_{\text{nh }$ become parallel to one another \cite{Flore, Nori, Bagarello, Murch,hatano2019exceptional .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The first major result of this Letter is to demonstrate that for pure decay systems (defined below), the Lindblad approximation is not valid outside the weak and singular coupling limits.
elements.sent-2411-019: For brevity, we will refer to this as R1.
elements.sent-2411-020: A second result of our work (R2) is to prove that for Lindblad dynamics with pure decay, quantum jumps can be ignored in the subspace with the highest particle number.
elements.sent-2411-021: This provides an exact equivalence between Lindblad dynamics and non-Hermitian dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our third major result (R3) is to demonstrate that for pure decay systems, exceptional points can exist only in the singular coupling limit and not in the weak coupling limit.
elements.sent-2411-023: This means that the intrinsic timescale of the isolated system must be comparable to the relaxation time of the system.
elements.sent-2411-024: This result will help guide experimental searches for open systems with exceptional points.
elements.sent-2411-025: If the bath modelling is appropriate for the system, {\em i.e. all the eigenstates of the isolated system overlap with the bath spectrum, then the number of particles in the system vanishes as $t\rightarrow \infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We call such an open system, and any other system that can be mapped onto it, a pure decay system.

elements.sent-2411-094: To summarize, a necessary condition for an exceptional point to appear is that the baths coupled to the system must be in the singular limit $B/A\rightarrow \infty$ and $C/A\rightarrow \infty$ with $\Gamma = C^2/B$ finite.
elements.sent-2411-095: This puts a strong constraint on experimental searches for exceptional points in open systems, given that it is more customary to design systems that are weakly-coupled to baths.
elements.sent-2411-096: Furthermore, we show that Lindblad and non-Hermitian dynamics are equivalent in the subspace with the highest occupation number.
elements.sent-2411-097: Taken together, these rule out the possibility of describing the evolution of a pure decay system with a non-Hermitian operator for any coupling that is neither weak nor singular.
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, we show that exceptional points can only arise for singular couplings.
elements.sent-2411-099: While there are still many open questions, such as what replaces the Lindblad master equation for couplings outsides these two limits, and whether these bounds also hold for systems with both decay and gain, our work advances our understanding of the valid descriptions of open systems and puts it on a more rigorous base.

elements.sent-2411-150: One well established approach for modelling open systems is the Lindblad master equation \cite{Lindblad1976, GKS, manzano2020short , which replaces solving the Schr\"odinger equation for the total Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{Open} H=H_A+H_B+H_C\end{equation} where $H_A$, $H_B$ and $H_C$ describe the system, the bath, and their coupling, respectively, with the Lindblad equation for the density matrix of the system \begin{equation}\label{Lindblad} \dot{\rho}_A=L[\rho_A]= -\im [H_A,\rho_A] + \sum_j \Gamma_j \left( L_j \rho_A L_j^\dag -\frac{1}{2} \left\{ L_j^\dag L_j , \rho_A \right\} \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-151: The Lindblad master equation is used to describe an open quantum system connected to a Markovian (memoryless) bath.
elements.sent-2411-152: The predictions of this master equations have been shown to be accurate (i) in the weak coupling limit, where the energy scale $C$ of the system-bath coupling is much smaller than the energy scales $A, B$ of the system and of the bath; and (ii) in the singular limit where $B/A\rightarrow \infty$ and $C/A\rightarrow \infty$ such that $C^2/B$ is constant \cite{BreuerPetruccione,palmer1977singular .}
